questions.question: What does `git clone` do?
options: Creates a local copy of a remote repository on your machine
Creates a copy of a repo under your own GitHub account
Downloads only the latest commit from the remote
Copies a local repository to a remote server
questions.answer: Creates a local copy of a remote repository on your machine

questions.question: What is the difference between `git clone` and a GitHub fork?
options: Clone creates a local copy; fork creates a copy under your GitHub account
Fork creates a local copy; clone creates a copy under your GitHub account
They are the same operation with different names
Clone only works with public repos; fork works with private repos
questions.answer: Clone creates a local copy; fork creates a copy under your GitHub account

questions.question: What does `git fetch` do?
options: Downloads new data from the remote but does NOT modify your working files
Downloads new data and merges it into your current branch
Uploads your local changes to the remote
Deletes remote-tracking branches that no longer exist
questions.answer: Downloads new data from the remote but does NOT modify your working files

questions.question: What is `git pull` equivalent to?
options: `git fetch` followed by `git merge`
`git fetch` followed by `git rebase`
`git clone` followed by `git merge`
`git push` followed by `git fetch`
questions.answer: `git fetch` followed by `git merge`

questions.question: Why is `git fetch` considered safer than `git pull`?
options: Because fetch does not modify your working files, letting you inspect changes first
Because fetch encrypts the data during transfer
Because fetch only downloads metadata, not actual file content
Because fetch automatically creates a backup branch
questions.answer: Because fetch does not modify your working files, letting you inspect changes first

questions.question: After cloning a repository, what is the default name of the remote?
options: origin
upstream
remote
main
questions.answer: origin

questions.question: Which command shows the remote URLs configured for your repository?
options: `git remote -v`
`git remote --list`
`git url --show`
`git config --remotes`
questions.answer: `git remote -v`

questions.question: After running `git clone`, which of the following is true about the cloned repository?
options: It has the full commit history and origin is already configured
It only has the latest commit and no remote configured
It has the full history but no remote is configured
It only has the files without any Git history
questions.answer: It has the full commit history and origin is already configured

questions.question: What does `git branch -a` show?
options: All branches, including remote-tracking branches
Only local branches
Only remote-tracking branches
Only the current branch
questions.answer: All branches, including remote-tracking branches

questions.question: After running `git fetch origin`, you notice `origin/main` is ahead of your local `main`. What has happened to your working directory?
options: Nothing has changed in your working directory
The new files have been added to your working directory
Your working directory has been updated to match origin/main
Your local commits have been replaced by the remote commits
questions.answer: Nothing has changed in your working directory

questions.question: After fetching, which command applies the downloaded changes to your local branch?
options: `git merge origin/main`
`git apply origin/main`
`git fetch --apply`
`git update origin/main`
questions.answer: `git merge origin/main`

questions.question: In the Day 9 simulation, what do the two local copies of the repository represent?
options: Two developers working on the same remote repository
A production server and a staging server
Two different branches of the same project
A backup copy and a working copy
questions.answer: Two developers working on the same remote repository

questions.question: What is the correct syntax to clone a repository into a specific folder name?
options: `git clone <url> <folder-name>`
`git clone --dir <folder-name> <url>`
`git clone <url> --output <folder-name>`
`git clone --name <folder-name> <url>`
questions.answer: `git clone <url> <folder-name>`

questions.question: After Developer A pushes a new file and Developer B runs `git fetch origin`, what can Developer B see?
options: The new commit in `origin/main` via `git log --all`, but the file is not in the working directory
The new file in the working directory immediately
Nothing, because fetch does not download any data
A conflict warning message
questions.answer: The new commit in `origin/main` via `git log --all`, but the file is not in the working directory

questions.question: What is the basic collaboration cycle described in Day 9?
options: Push from one side, pull from the other
Fork, clone, then submit a pull request
Create a branch, merge, then delete the branch
Fetch, rebase, then force push
questions.answer: Push from one side, pull from the other

questions.question: Which command shows the commit history as a graph with all branches?
options: `git log --oneline --graph --all`
`git history --graph --all`
`git log --tree --branches`
`git show --graph --oneline`
questions.answer: `git log --oneline --graph --all`

questions.question: When you clone a repository, what are the branches listed under `remotes/origin/` called?
options: Remote-tracking branches
Feature branches
Detached branches
Mirror branches
questions.answer: Remote-tracking branches

questions.question: Developer B makes a commit in the clone and runs `git push`. What must Developer A do to see that commit locally?
options: Run `git pull` (or `git fetch` then `git merge`)
Run `git clone` again
Run `git push` to sync
Run `git commit --sync`
questions.answer: Run `git pull` (or `git fetch` then `git merge`)

questions.question: When is it appropriate to use `git pull` instead of the safer fetch-then-merge workflow?
options: For simple cases when you are confident there will be no conflicts
When you want to discard your local changes
When working on a shared branch with many developers
Never; fetch-then-merge should always be used
questions.answer: For simple cases when you are confident there will be no conflicts

questions.question: What does `git log --oneline` display?
options: Each commit on a single line showing the abbreviated hash and message
Only the most recent commit in full detail
The list of files changed in each commit
The full commit hash with author and date
questions.answer: Each commit on a single line showing the abbreviated hash and message

questions.question: If you run `git pull` and there are no new commits on the remote, what happens?
options: Git reports that your branch is already up to date
Git creates an empty merge commit
Git throws an error because there is nothing to pull
Git resets your branch to the remote state
questions.answer: Git reports that your branch is already up to date

questions.question: In the Day 9 exercise, after running `git fetch origin` from the clone, which command confirms that the fetched file is NOT yet in your working directory?
options: `ls`
`git status`
`git diff`
`git show`
questions.answer: `ls`

questions.question: What does `git push` do after you commit changes in the cloned repository?
options: Uploads your local commits to the remote repository
Downloads changes from the remote repository
Creates a new branch on the remote
Merges the remote branch into your local branch
questions.answer: Uploads your local commits to the remote repository

questions.question: After both developers have pushed and pulled changes, what does `git log --oneline --graph --all` show?
options: Both developers' commits interleaved in a unified history
Only the commits made by the current developer
Two separate histories that are not connected
Only the merge commits, not the individual commits
questions.answer: Both developers' commits interleaved in a unified history

questions.question: A fork is a feature of which platform, not a built-in Git command?
options: GitHub
Git itself
The terminal shell
The operating system
questions.answer: GitHub
